questions.text: In the von Neumann stored-program concept, instructions and data are…
choices: stored in separate, physically distinct memories
stored in the same memory, distinguished only by context
never stored in memory at the same time
always encoded in different number bases
questions.explanation: The defining feature of the von Neumann architecture is that instructions and data share one memory and are transferred over the same buses; the CPU tells them apart only by how it is currently using them.

questions.text: Which register holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched?
questions.explanation: The Program Counter (PC) holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched, and is incremented after each fetch.

questions.text: Which register holds the instruction currently being decoded and executed?
questions.explanation: The Current Instruction Register (CIR) holds the instruction the Control Unit is currently decoding/executing, after it has been copied there from the MDR during fetch.

questions.text: Which of these registers hold a memory **address** (rather than a data value or instruction)?
questions.explanation: PC holds the address of the next instruction and MAR holds the address currently being read from/written to. ACC holds calculation results and MDR holds the data/instruction value being transferred — both values, not addresses.

questions.text: Which of the following are stages of the fetch-decode-execute cycle?
questions.explanation: The cycle has exactly three stages: fetch, decode and execute. Compiling turns source code into machine code and happens separately, long before the program is loaded and run.

questions.text: During the fetch stage, which bus carries the actual instruction value back to the CPU?
choices: There is no such bus in this model
questions.explanation: The address bus carries the address to be read (from the MAR); the value stored there travels back to the CPU on the data bus, into the MDR. The control bus only carries coordination signals such as read/write.

questions.text: Which of the following are factors that directly affect CPU performance, as covered in this module?
choices: The colour of the CPU's case
questions.explanation: Clock speed, number of cores and cache size are the three examinable performance factors — a higher clock speed completes more cycles per second, more cores enable parallel work, and more cache reduces slow trips to main memory. Case colour has no effect on performance.

questions.text: Which level of cache is typically the smallest and fastest, built directly into each core?
questions.explanation: L1 cache is the smallest and fastest cache level, sitting closest to the core; L2 is larger and slower, L3 larger and slower still, and RAM (main memory) is slowest of all.

questions.text: Give the term for a program's tendency to reuse the same, or nearby, memory locations repeatedly over a short period of time.
questions.explanation: This property is called **locality of reference** (temporal locality: reusing the same location soon; spatial locality: reusing nearby locations soon). It is why cache memory is effective.

questions.text: For a half-adder with inputs $A = 1$ and $B = 1$, what is the value of the **sum** output?
questions.explanation: Sum $= A \oplus B$ (XOR). $1 \oplus 1 = 0$ — the inputs are equal, so XOR outputs 0. This matches binary addition: $1+1=10_2$, a sum digit of 0 with a carry.

questions.text: For a half-adder with inputs $A = 1$ and $B = 1$, what is the value of the **carry** output?
questions.explanation: Carry $= A \cdot B$ (AND). $1 \cdot 1 = 1$ — both inputs are 1, so a carry is generated, matching $1+1=10_2$.
